en.extract:
The fulvous owl (Strix fulvescens), or Guatemala barred owl, is a resident of the cloud forests of Central America. A medium-sized true owl, it has a round head, lacking ear tufts. Its typical coloration is warm dark brown or reddish brown on the back and lighter brown on the front with darker barring. Adults weigh approximately 600 grams (21 oz), with females being heavier. Its distribution is limited to highland regions of Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador. It inhabits elevations from 1,200 to 3,100 meters (3,900 to 10,200 ft), and is fairly common within its range. Its behavior is poorly known, as are its population size and distribution. It is classified as a species of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature, although it is considered endangered in Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
The fulvous owl is a monotypic species in the genus Strix. It is a member of the true owl family, Strigidae. It was formally described in 1868, as Syrnium fulvescens. The type specimen came from Guatemala. In 1970 it was classified as a subspecies of the barred owl (Strix varia), but it is generally recognized as a separate species today, although some researchers consider it part of a superspecies, along with the barred owl and possibly the spotted owl (Strix occidentalis). The fulvous owl is sometimes known as the Guatemalan barred owl.


Description
The fulvous owl is a medium-sized owl with a round head. It lacks the ear tufts found in many other owls. Its length is variously described as between 41 and 44 centimetres (16+1⁄8 and 17+3⁄8 in), between 38 and 48.5 centimetres (15 and 19+1⁄8 in), and between 40.5 and 45 centimetres (16 and 17+3⁄4 in). Measured wing lengths for the species range between 30 and 33.3 centimetres (11+3⁄4 and 13+1⁄8 in), while tail length ranges between 18.5 and 21 centimetres (7+1⁄4 and 8+1⁄4 in), and the bill length between 22.5 and 24.5 millimetres (7⁄8 and 31⁄32 in). Adults weigh approximately 600 grams (21 oz), with females on average being 100 grams (3.5 oz) heavier.
The facial disc of the species is a dull brownish-white or pale ochre in color, and is slightly darker around the eyes. The disc has concentric dark brown bars around it, and short white eyebrows. The crown, the nape of the neck, and the upperparts of the species are a rich, warm dark brown or reddish brown. The crown and the neck have scalloped markings in a lighter shade. The upperparts of the bird are covered by sparse bars colored "ochraceous buff", that become smaller and less regular on the scapulars, lower back, and rump. The wing coverts have small pale brown markings, while the greater and median coverts have a white spot near the base. The primaries have big spots ranging from whitish to pale brown. The secondaries have five or six light brown bands. The underparts of the bird are shades of light brown, with dark brown or reddish-brown streaks. The undertail coverts are buff colored, with some dark streaks on the longest feathers. The bill and the cere are yellowish in color. The legs are covered in reddish-brown feathering till the toes, which are yellowish. The claws are horn-colored with dark tips. The iris is dark brown.
Sexes are alike in the fulvous owl. Chicks are whitish, while juveniles are cinnamon-brown with yellowish or white barring and a brownish facial disc. The species is visually most similar to the barred owl, found in Mexico, Canada, and the United States. The barred owl is more gray above and more white below. The fulvous owl is approximately one-fifth smaller than the Mexican variant of the barred owl. The mottled owl (Ciccaba virgata) has a similar range as the fulvous owl, but is found only at lower altitudes. It is darker brown and smaller, while the facial disc is dark with a white edge, as opposed to light with a dark edge in the fulvous owl.


Distribution and habitat
The fulvous owl is found in highland regions in the Mexican states of Chiapas and Oaxaca, Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador. Its existence in Oaxaca was reported in 1950, but locations from which the specimens were collected had been challenged. The species was confirmed to exist in Oaxaca in 2011. Its range is poorly known, but thought to be large. A montane species, it is found at altitudes between 1,200 and 3,100 meters (3,900 and 10,200 ft) above sea level. The species is found in montane evergreen pine forests and humid pine-oak forests, and more generally within a cloud forest habitat. The International Union for Conservation of Nature estimates that the population of the species is between 20,000 and 50,000 individuals, although the population is suspected to be declining as a result of habitat loss. As a result of its large range and substantial population, it is classified as a species of least concern by the IUCN. The species is estimated to have declined by half in Mexico over the last century as a result of habitat loss, and the Mexican government consider it an endangered species. The species was traditionally considered a messenger of death in parts of Mexico.


Behavior and ecology
The call of the fulvous owl is described as a loud barking hoot, rendered as "who-wuhu-woot-woot" or "a'hoo a'hoo-hoo a'hoo, hoo": the number of notes is variable. The rhythm of the call has been likened to that of Morse code. The call of the female is higher in pitch, and is sometimes uttered in a duet with the male. The call lacks the terminal note of the barred owl call. Other vocalizations uttered by the species have been described as "parrot-like, nasal gwao calls, singly or in series, as well as single hoots". The species is nocturnal, though it occasionally calls during the day, and hunts from a perch. Its diet is poorly known. It likely consists of rodents, large insects, birds, frogs, and lizards. The stomach of a dissected specimen was found to contain "large insects". An individual was observed attempting to catch a highland guan, while the feathers of a blue-throated motmot were found below the nest of another.
The fulvous owl does not migrate. Very little information exists about the behavior of this species. It is presumed to be monogamous, as with most owl species. Young of the species have been observed in May. The incubation period is between 28 and 30 days long, and the number of eggs in a clutch varies between two and five, but generally is either two or three. Nests are usually made in holes or cavities in trees. Females incubate the eggs, and males are thought to forage for the females and young.

The '''fulvous owl''' ('''''Strix fulvescens'''''), or '''Guatemala barred owl''', is a resident of the [[cloud forest]]s of [[Central America]]. A medium-sized [[true owl]], it has a round head, lacking [[ear tuft]]s. Its typical coloration is warm dark brown or reddish brown on the back and lighter brown on the front with darker barring. Adults weigh approximately {{convert|600|g}}, with females being heavier. Its distribution is limited to highland regions of [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], and [[El Salvador]]. It inhabits elevations from {{convert|1200|to|3100|meters}}, and is fairly common within its range. Its behavior is poorly known, as are its population size and distribution. It is classified as a species of [[least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]], although it is considered endangered in Mexico.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The fulvous owl is a [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] species in the genus ''[[Strix (genus)|Strix]]''.<ref name="Mikkola2017">{{cite book|first=Heimo|last=Mikkola|title=Owls of the World – A Photographic Guide|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NXQ4DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA345|year=2017|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-4561-7|page=345}}</ref> It is a member of the [[true owl]] family, Strigidae. It was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1868, as ''Syrnium fulvescens''. The [[type specimen]] came from Guatemala.<ref name="Systematics">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Systematics|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/systematics |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> In 1970 it was classified as a subspecies of the [[barred owl]] (''Strix varia''), but it is generally recognized as a separate species today, although some researchers consider it part of a [[superspecies]], along with the barred owl and possibly the [[spotted owl]] (''Strix occidentalis'').<ref name="Systematics"/> The fulvous owl is sometimes known as the Guatemalan barred owl.<ref name="Marcot 1995">{{cite book|author1=Bruce G. Marcot|title=Owls of old forests of the world|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YA66_bIEVrAC|year=1995|publisher=U.S. Dept. of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Northwest Research Station|page=54}}</ref>

==Description==
The fulvous owl is a medium-sized owl with a round head. It lacks the [[ear tuft]]s found in many other owls.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/> Its length is variously described as between {{convert|41|and|44|cm|in|frac=8}}, between {{convert|38|and|48.5|cm|in|frac=8}},<ref name="Appearance">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Appearance |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/appearance |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> and between {{convert|40.5|and|45|cm|in|frac=8}}.<ref name=HBW>{{cite encyclopedia|last1=Holt|first1=D.W.|last2=Berkley|first2=R.|last3=Deppe|first3=C.|last4=Enríquez Rocha|first4=P.|last5=Petersen|first5=J.L.|last6=Rangel Salazar|first6=J.L.|last7=Segars|first7=K.P.|last8=Wood|first8=K.L.|last9=Marks|first9=J.S.|year=2019|title=Fulvous Owl (Strix fulvescens)|editor-last1=del Hoyo|editor-first1=J.|editor-last2=Elliott|editor-first2=A.|editor-last3=Sargatal|editor-first3=J.|editor-last4=Christie|editor-first4=D.A.|editor-last5=de Juana|editor-first5=E.|encyclopedia=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive|publisher=Lynx Edicions|location=Barcelona, Spain|url=https://www.hbw.com/species/fulvous-owl-strix-fulvescens|access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> Measured wing lengths for the species range between {{convert|30|and|33.3|cm|in|frac=8}}, while tail length ranges between {{convert|18.5|and|21|cm|in|frac=8}}, and the bill length between {{convert|22.5|and|24.5|mm|in|frac=32}}.<ref name="Appearance"/> Adults weigh approximately {{convert|600|g}}, with females on average being {{convert|100|g}} heavier.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/>

The [[facial disc]] of the species is a dull brownish-white or pale ochre in color, and is slightly darker around the eyes. The disc has concentric dark brown bars around it, and short white eyebrows. The [[Crown (anatomy)|crown]], the nape of the neck, and the upperparts of the species are a rich, warm dark brown or reddish brown. The crown and the neck have scalloped markings in a lighter shade. The upperparts of the bird are covered by sparse bars colored "ochraceous buff", that become smaller and less regular on the [[scapulars]], lower back, and rump. The [[wing coverts]] have small pale brown markings, while the greater and median coverts have a white spot near the base. The [[Primary feathers|primaries]] have big spots ranging from whitish to pale brown. The [[Secondary feathers|secondaries]] have five or six light brown bands. The underparts of the bird are shades of light brown, with dark brown or reddish-brown streaks. The [[Covert feather|undertail coverts]] are buff colored, with some dark streaks on the longest feathers.<ref name="Appearance"/> The bill and the [[Beak#Cere|cere]] are yellowish in color. The legs are covered in reddish-brown feathering till the toes, which are yellowish. The claws are horn-colored with dark tips.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/> The [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] is dark brown.<ref name="Appearance" />

Sexes are alike in the fulvous owl. Chicks are whitish, while juveniles are cinnamon-brown with yellowish or white barring and a brownish facial disc. The species is visually most similar to the barred owl, found in Mexico, Canada, and the United States. The barred owl is more gray above and more white below. The fulvous owl is approximately one-fifth smaller than the Mexican variant of the barred owl. The [[mottled owl]] (''Ciccaba virgata'') has a similar range as the fulvous owl, but is found only at lower altitudes. It is darker brown and smaller, while the facial disc is dark with a white edge, as opposed to light with a dark edge in the fulvous owl.<ref name="Appearance" />

==Distribution and habitat==
The fulvous owl is found in highland regions in the Mexican states of [[Chiapas]] and [[Oaxaca]], Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/><ref name=HBW/><ref name="Distribution">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Distribution |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/distribution |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> Its existence in Oaxaca was reported in 1950, but locations from which the specimens were collected had been challenged. The species was confirmed to exist in Oaxaca in 2011.<ref name="Distribution"/> Its range is poorly known, but thought to be large. A [[montane]] species, it is found at altitudes between {{convert|1200|and|3100|meters}} above sea level.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name="Distribution"/> The species is found in montane evergreen pine forests and humid pine-oak forests,<ref name="Mikkola2017"/> and more generally within a [[cloud forest]] habitat.<ref name="Distribution"/> The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] estimates that the population of the species is between 20,000 and 50,000 individuals, although the population is suspected to be declining as a result of habitat loss. As a result of its large range and substantial population, it is classified as a species of least concern by the IUCN.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> The species is estimated to have declined by half in Mexico over the last century as a result of habitat loss, and the Mexican government consider it an endangered species.<ref name="Conservation">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Conservation |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/conservation |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> The species was traditionally considered a messenger of death in parts of Mexico.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Josabad Alonso-Castro |first1=Angel |title=Use of medicinal fauna in Mexican traditional medicine |journal=Journal of Ethnopharmacology |volume=152 |issue=1 |pages=53–70 |doi=10.1016/j.jep.2014.01.005 |pmid=24440438 |year=2014 }}</ref>

==Behavior and ecology==
The call of the fulvous owl is described as a loud barking hoot, rendered as "who-wuhu-woot-woot" or "a'hoo a'hoo-hoo a'hoo, hoo": the number of notes is variable.<ref name=HBW/> The rhythm of the call has been likened to that of [[Morse code]]. The call of the female is higher in pitch, and is sometimes uttered in a duet with the male. The call lacks the terminal note of the barred owl call. Other vocalizations uttered by the species have been described as "parrot-like, nasal gwao calls, singly or in series, as well as single hoots".<ref name="Sounds">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Sounds and Vocal Behavior |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/sounds |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> The species is [[nocturnal]], though it occasionally calls during the day, and hunts from a perch. Its diet is poorly known.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/><ref name="HBW" /><ref name="Behavior">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Behavior |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/behavior |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> It likely consists of rodents, large insects, birds, frogs, and lizards.<ref name="Mikkola2017"/><ref name="HBW" /> The stomach of a dissected specimen was found to contain "large insects". An individual was observed attempting to catch a [[highland guan]], while the feathers of a [[blue-throated motmot]] were found below the nest of another.<ref name="Diet">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Diet and Foraging |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/foodhabits |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref>

The fulvous owl does not migrate.<ref name="HBW" /> Very little information exists about the behavior of this species. It is presumed to be monogamous, as with most owl species.<ref name="Behavior"/> Young of the species have been observed in May. The incubation period is between 28 and 30 days long, and the number of eggs in a clutch varies between two and five, but generally is either two or three. Nests are usually made in holes or cavities in trees.<ref name="HBW" /><ref name="Breeding">{{cite web |last1=Sawa |first1=Erin |title=Fulvous Owl Strix fulvescens – Breeding |url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/fulowl1/breeding |publisher=Cornell Ornithology Lab |access-date=8 January 2019}}</ref> Females incubate the eggs, and males are thought to forage for the females and young.<ref name="Breeding"/>

zh.extract:
茶色林鴞（學名Strix fulvescens）是一種棲息在中美洲雲林內的貓頭鷹。它們的棲息地只限於高海拔的山區，由1200-3000米，包括瓜地馬拉、洪都拉斯及薩爾瓦多的雲林。雖然它們主要是夜間活動的，但有時也可在日間見到它們。
